CURRENT AFFAIRS,

e

The Belfast News Letter is still screeching, Here is
how its leading article of June 21st commences:—" Tho
Nationalist membors of Parliament, by their opposition
to the Great Northern Railway (Ircland) Bill and other
private Bills, are showing their disregard of tho real
interests of the country which they profess to serve.
They are now, as they have always been, the representa-
tives of the Roman Catholic Church rather than of the
Trish people,and they aro concerned more about sectarian
aggrandisemont than national prosperity.” That is all
tho thanks they get, even though Mr. John Dillon,
whilst he held so many shares as would entitle him t-
be a director of the Great Northern Bigot Railway,

never lifted even his voice on the floor of the Great
Northern Railway Board room.

Listen to this concerning the request for a system of
competitive examinations for the clerkships of the Bigot
Railway : —“ The demand is a part of the agitation
which was organised somo time ago by the notorious
“ Catholic Association,” or by persons in sympathy with
it. Tho object is to bring sectarianism into everything,
and to make a man’s fitness for a commercial appoint-
ment depend on the religion which he professes.””  The
poor old Orange organ must be stung to fury at the
very idea of partially open competition free to the
Christian Brothers’ pupils. So the object of open com-
petition is to bring “sectarianism into everything!”” What
a dreadfully “sectarian’’ institution his Most Gracious
Majesty’s Civil Service must be.  Mr. Sloan should
raiso this matter in the House, and see that the Civil
Servico of the country is staffed on strictly  non-sec-
tarian ’’ principles by a joint Board composed of the
Belfast Corporation, the Rathmines Urban Councillors
and the directors of the Great Northern Non-secta-
rian ”’ Railway.

Hero is a further gem from the Beljast News Let
“ The reason for the demand is one which the di
cannot accept. They have never made appointments on
religious grounds. When vacancies occur they do not
ask the religion of candidates, but they inquire into
their fitness for the work which they have to do; and
they cannot admit, even to conciliate the Nationalists,
that appointments are influenced by sectarian preju-
dices.”” We commend this gem to those honest people
who, in the simplicity of their hearts, think that we
are “sectarian’’ because of the almost single-handed
fight we have fought all those years for Justice, Toler-
ance and Fair Play. The negotiations and the break-
ing off of them by the bigots of the Great Northern
Railway show the necessity of strengthening the Catho-
lic Defence Society.

The Limerick Shirt Factory, St. Ita’s, is now com-
paratively quite a big concern, and it has a promising
future. We personally can bear testimony to the shirts,
for we got two of them about a year ago, and they are
excellent in every way. The Limerick Shirt Factory had
a small beginning. It owes its establishment to the
initiative and energy of Father Cregan, the adminis-
trator of St. Michael’s, in which parish the Rector of the
Irish Collego at Rome, “ M. O’R.,”” was for many years
a hard-working curate. Father Cregan started the in-
dustry originally a few years ago on £100 capital. Three
workers and a forewoman was the extent of the staff;
there aro thirty-five girls now employed. In the mean-
time, however, the concern had been turned into a Limi-
ted Liability Company and increased capital procured.
Alderman O’Mara is one of the directors, and: Mr.
0’Kane, a widely experienced and practical man in the
shirt industry, is managor. The machines aro run by
cloctric current which the Limerick Corporation supply
at the oxtraordinary low price of 13d. a unit. Said
‘Alderman O’Mara:— Limerick men ofton abused the
Corporation—and perhaps with reason—but there was
this to say for them, that that Corporation c_ould s“}?ply
motor power cheaper than any other place in the king-
dom. Ho had some knowledge of motor power and
machinery, and he didn’t know that even in America
motor power could be sold at so low a price as the Cor-
poration of Limerick sell it.”’ Alderman O Mar@ ap-
pealod for public support for tho products of St. Ita’s
factory, but he does not ask the pul.)h.c to sacrifice even
as much as & brass farthing in giving thab support.
“They did not want,’”” said he, *“ any compliment from
anybody. They wanted simply preference, and if they
could not supply as good and as cheap an article to the

‘public they didn’t want the public to buy from them,
but they would ask the public in Limerick to give them
the preference of their money.”” By tho way, Father
Crogan, in relating briefly the early history of the in-
dustry, referred to the help which Father Creagh
through the Confraternity gave it. Ho said :— Some
time after the Exhibition Father Croagh, who did so
much for Limerick, happened to bo up abt the station
house when the train came in from tho West of Ireland,
.and he was surprised to see the large crowd of girls going
from that train and proceeding to the platform from
which the Cork train starts.  They were going to
America. Several told him they had no work to do
" here. He (Father Creagh) encouraged tho men of tho
Confraternity to help on local industries, and try and
do what they could to stop emigration. He encouraged
the Confraternity men in a special manner to buy the
shirts from St. Ita’s, and to ask for them in the various
shops. That was such help to them that they were even
yet finding the offect of the great work Father Creagh

$ did for them.” v
It so happens that a fow days ago an Ulster lady
wrote to us suggesting that ladies’ Confraternities might
be spoken to with a view to oxtending a market for
TIrish industries. She instanced the “Children of Mary,”
and asks, “ could it be arranged that some Irish-Ireland
priests in each Diocese would be allowed to address these
meetings of women, twice or thrice a year? They
could speak plainly, as the missioners do, to them show-
ing how it is each one’s duty to try to save the young
men and women from emigration and its evils, by each
woman resolving to buy only Irish-made goods where
possible. T think you will see how this idea might be
worked out; and the men’s Confraternities could also be

addressed regularly.”’

The Waterford Technical Instruction Committee on
Friday last elected a head master for the new schools.
Mr. O’Shaughnessy was elected by seventeen votes to
three, as against a Mr. Smith, who, we believe, is an
importation from Great Britain residing some years in
Treland. Tt is a fact worth noting that all the Pro-
testants present—Sir W. G. D. Goff, Alderman W. R.
Ward, High Sheriff, and Mr. A. Nelson—voted for the
Briton. Mr. Nelson, it may be remembered, gained a
little notoriety some years ago by refusing the local
Court House to the Gaelic League. All the Catholics
present, with the exception of his lordship the Bishop,
the Most Rev. Dr. Sheehan, who did not vote at all,
voted for Mr. O’Shaughnessy. The appointment of a
manual Instructor, on the proposition of Dr. O’Sulli-
van, was postponed, though Mr. Nelson, the West Briton
who had the row with the Gaelic League, was anxious
to have the vacancy filled there and then. An adver-
tisement concerning this vacancy will be found in
another column, and we hope that plenty of Irish appli-
cants will have a try for the position.

There is an industrial association in Galway now,
.and, largely as a result of the energy and initiative of
, Father Glendon, O.P., there is a project in the hatch-
ing for the betterment of the Claddagh. Thero is also
the Galway Granite and Marble Works, of which tho
“Hon. Captain Campbell and Mr. Martin M‘Donagh are
directors. We have before us a type-written circular
asking for subscriptions for the Connaught Provincial
Horse and Cattle Show, which is to be held on the 18th
and 19th July. Wae sec that subscriptions may be for-
warded to, amongst others, the two directors of the Gal-
way Granite and Marble Works whose names we have
mentioned. On turning the paper, upon which the cir-
. cular is typed, to the light, wo find that tho watermark
is “James and Sons. Fine.”” From an Irish indus-
trial point of view we submit that it is anything but
fine. Why did not the “ Connaught Provincial Horse
and Cattle Show ” print their circular on Irish-made
paper? Could they not get it on satisfactory terms?

The arrangements for the projected boot factory on
American lines in Dublin are going on nicely and with-
out a hitch, We understand that about two-thirds of the

THE LEADER.

JuNE 30, 1906.

capital necessary to start the concern has already been
promised. An influential Irishman, resident in Eng-
land, writing to a friend in Ireland, gives an account of
a conversation he had with a member of a large English
boot firm. The Irishman “drew ’’ the English boot
man on the subject of the projected boot factory in
Dublin.  The Englishman’s opinion was that things
were in a dangerous state in Ireland! He nover likes
to seo Ireland quiet, for that, in his view, means mis-
chief. The more the people here merely clamour for
Home Rule and have rows, the better he thinks for
English manufacure; but this Indusbria_l Revival meant
a big loss to English manufactures. His travellers told
him that unless something is donc promptly there is
overy likelihood of a factory being started in Dublin.
Wo wonder what are they likely to do, either promptly
or remotely, that could hinder the boot, factory in Dub-
lin? To undersell the output of the factory would
necessitate the establishment of a kind of Trust amongst
English boot manufacturers, and that is not likely to bo
formed. This English bootman fears that factories will
be starting up everywhere in Ireland, and that British
trade will be injured. How soon this Englishman looks
out in the old Repressive Law spirit on Ireland when
any one says Industries. However, though injury to Eng-
lish trade as a consequence of Irish Industrial Revival
would not fret us in the least, we do not agree that a
rise of industrial prosperity in Ireland would necessarily
injure British trade with Ireland. If we all rode Irish-
made bicycles, no doubt the British bicycle manufac-
tures’ Irish trade would he hit; but, taking the trade in
the lump, we are rather inclined to think that if Ire-
land increased her industrial output tenfold, that Eng-
lish trade with Ireland would increase rather than di-
minish. Be that as it may, we hope the boot factory
project will shortly materialise so as to give a start to
the English manufacturer who is suspicious of Ireland
quiet, and would prefer Ireland noisy.

The second Perr Citte Moceattog (Kilmallock,
where the athletes come from), will be held to-morrow.
(Friday).  His Lordship the Bishop of Limerick is
Patron, and Father James O’Shea, P.P., is President
of the feip. Amongst the Vice-Presidents, General
Committee and Organising Committee, we count nine-
teen ““Idolatrous’’ priests, but we do not see the name
of any minister of the “ Church of Ireland.”  Might
not Mr. Stephen Gwynn go down to this place, and
use up some of his oratorical energy in view of this
state of affairs? In connection with the perp, the Kil-
mallock industrial associations have organised a series
of competitions in lace, woollen and fancy work and
plain sewing. We also note that there is a prize for
the best drawing done by a school-boy of any Ivish
subject.

In our present issue the Midland Great Western
Railway Co. advertise a cheap Sunday trip to Sligo. On
Sunday, Ist July, an excursion train will leave Dublin
at 8 a.m. for Sligo, returning at 6.30 p.m. The fares
for the return journcy being: Ist class, 10s.; 2nd class,
Ts. 6d.; 3rd class, Bs.

I'reeman, no doubt, owing to a fit of absent-mindedness,

clipped it.

One of the features at the Maynooth Union this
year was a paper in Irish by Rev. Dr. Henebry, fol-
lowed by a discussion wholly in Irish. The Cardinal,
in the course of his opening address, said:—

There was another maiter in which he was deeply intorested—
though he had done very littlo for the cause no ono was moro
deeply intercited in it than he was himsolf—that was the success
of the movement which had taken hold of the people so strongly—
the movement for the rovival of their old tongue (applausc). Ho
thought the meeings of the Maynooth Union might contribute vory
much to promote that great object, as well as many other objects.
With a view to promoting that object it would bo arranged, as
announced, that when the Irish papers wero being read to-day
1o one would be permitted to speak a word except liish.  Anyone
desiring to speak on the papers who could not make his remarks
in Irish, must keep quict and hold h's tongue.

And in the course of the Sccretary’s report, read by
Reav. Dr. Harty, we read:—

Acting in harmony with the Irish spirit which now pervades
our race, we have devoted one of our Sectional Meectings to an
Irish paper. No word of English is to be spoken in the hall,
where Dr. O'Donnell presides and Dr. Henebry delivers his lecture.
We hopa that the good work now begun will continue in future
vears, and will do honour to all our mcetings (applause).

An additional sum of £100 was voted to the Irish His-
torical Publication Committee.

The bitter indignation that is aroused by the treat-

ment accorded to Irish in the “ National ’’ schools was
given further expression to by Father Dinneen in a
rattling speech at the recent quarterly meecting of the
Keating Branch. Father Dinneen asked—“The Govern-
ment of this country regard Irish as a serious educa-
tional subject, or they do not? If they do not, why
not tell us so in plain language? Why the words of
sympathy and promise? Why try to gull the country
into the calmness of indifference? If they do regard it
as a serious subject, why not legislate for it seriously?
Why deal with it on the halfpenny-per-day-remunera-
tion principle? Why give it a nominal place in a hope-
lessly crowded programme? Why make teacher’s incre-
ments dependent on the biliousness of anti-Irish in-
spectors?  Why try to fool the public with offers of
paltry prizes for excellence in work which they so openly
ban and discourage? Why all this jugglery—why this
trifling with a subject which is so intimately bound up
with the nation’s aspirations and the nation’s hopes?”
Well, our answer to all these questions is—because the
Government would like to humbug us if it can. Eng-
land doesn’t want to be bothered with an Irish Ireland,
and the permanent officials of England in this country—
all that we sum up in the words “ Dublin Castlo”’—
are steadily opposed to Irish Ireland. Our business is
not to allow ourselves to be humbugged. And one way
to tone down the wrong and injustice that the Govern-
ment will do us if it can is, to keep on giving as much
real trouble as possible, by making ourselves as
great a nuisance as we can to the powers that be.  If
we were sabisfied with Mr. Bryce’s sympathy, no doubt,
he would reconsider his position, and in a short time
draw off even his supply of soft talk.
" The “ Irish ”’ Times even begrudges the financial pro-
visions for Irish in the “ National’’ Schools. In the
course of its leading article on Dr. Starkie, it says:—
“He (Mr. Bryce) leaves National schools in the state
described by Dr. Starkio, but he can find £6,000 per
annum to finance the teaching of the Irish language at
the bidding of the Gaelic League.”

Next week the Connacht Irish College, Mount Partry,
Co. Mayo, opens its first session. The Munster Train-
ing College (Ballingeary) opens on Monday. Next week
also the Irish school at Ring, Co. Waterford, opens,
and applications for admission, etc., may be sent to Mr.
John O’Flynn, Trish teacher, Carrick-on-Suir, Co. Tip-
perary.

We have received the following letter : —

The Amalgamated Society of Railway Servants
of England, Ireland, Scotland and Wales.
Head Offices: 72 Acton Street, Gray’s Inn
Road, London, W.C.

Telegraphic Address: “Beware, London.”
Excursion Traffic for G. S. and W.

8 Ballybricken, Waterford,
18th June, 1906.

Dear Sir—At our meeting last night, a discussion
took place on your comments on mine of 5th inst. re
above. And I had been instructed to inform you, with
a view to convincing you of our motive, that we ap-
proached, and sought to approach “Idolatrous’’
sodalities and ‘‘ Christian ’ associations with the view
to obtaining their patronage in the matter of excur-
sions for the G. S. and W., not as “Idolators,”’ not
as “ Christians,”” not as “ Home Rulers,” nor in the
capacity of servants or canvassers of the company, but
as you state in the capacity of members of the Water-
ford Branch of the A.S.R.8., and from that point of
view, which should be foremost in the minds of all
workers, “tho recognition of their respective trade
organisations by their employers,’”” or, in other words,
tho bread and butter point of view, this
latter  point of view specially appealing to
the men of the G. S. and W. at Waterford, seeing
the difference in wages and hours of duty between them
and the men of the D. W. and W., amongst whom
organisation is totally unknown, and I am sure you will
agreo with us that whero organisation does not exist
amongst any body of men, there can be no regularity in
wages or condibions of service, and that there is the
dlrmger of reduction for those who work in contact with
them,

As to bigotry, we fear it exists, to a great extent, on
all Trish railways, and until the affected parties recog-
niso that, in addition to the very powerful aid offered

them by the Press of the country and the clergy—heaven
helps those who help themselves—so long will such
- bigotry exist; but we would not, under any circum-
; .sta:nces, advise them to start a local defence society,
- but to grasp the already manufactured weapon of the
A.S.R.S. (with its fund at tho end of 1905
£330,567 10s. 9d.), or some kindred society.  How
powerful would be clerks and men of the rank and file
of any railway company if banded together in one solid
combination—the clerks, of course, working branches

As to the starfing of an Irish Railway Servants’ As-
sociation or trades union, we should like to know how
" long, and having such’ men as the employés of the
DRV V. to drag along, it would take to be in
such a position as the A.S.R.S. stands to-day, with its
fund, as already shown, and threo members in Parlia-
ment to directly represent the interests of railway men.
With its Orphan Fund, paying benefits amounting last
year to £9,516 8s. 3d. to 1,012 families of railwaymen,
and also a legal defence fund and suspension and dona-
tion funds. Such organisations only should be availed of,
for they aro not ono bit too powerful to cope with the
employers , of the present-day associations. Trusting
that much good may be the result of our controversy.—
I remain, yours truly,
M. O’CoNNOR.

We have nothing to say against organisation as such.
We believe in government, though we are strongly op-
posed to the British government of Ireland.
lieve in the organisation of Irish labour, though we are
against the domination of British organisations in Irish
labour as we are against the domination of the British
Parliament over Ireland. It seems to us to be no argu-
ment against Home Rule to ask the absurd question—
how long would it take Ireland to become as rich as
the United Kingdom? Nor. does it seem to us any
axgument against the desirability of an Irish organisa-
tion for Irish railway servants to ask when such an
organisation would be of the dimensions of the A.S.R.S.
We are sorry to hear that the D. W. and W. Railway
servants are not organised, and we trust that they will
set about organising themselves without delay. If we
would be able to govern ourselves from College Green,
how is it impossible for the servants of the Great Sour-
face and other railways to run their own distinctive
labour organisations? If we cannot run our own labour
organisations, how could we run our own government
if we got the chance? And if Irish labour men throw
up their hands at the task of organising Irish labour
as an entity, would it not be better for them to give
up all pretence to being Home Rulers? How could
bigotry, which admittedly exists on Irish railways, be
fought by Irish railway servants joining an interna-

CORRESPONDENCE.

EMIGRATION.

Sir,—In reading your issue of the LEADER of 1lth
June more minutely, I see that ‘ Northern Reader’
refers to the wages of farm labourers as from £16 to £20.
Will ke bo kind enough to state what are the ruling
wages for farm labourers in the North or in other parts
of Ireland. If £20 is given, surely you are right, sir, "
in your contention re staying at home.—I am, sir, yours,

INQUIRER,

AMERICAN MAGHINERY ADVERTISEMENTS.

Dear Sir—I have recently noticed a poster, issued by
an American firm of mower manufacturers, and I think
it is well, in the interests of Irish machines, to draw
attention to it.

This poster is headed in large type—‘ Ireland’s
Favourite,”” which words are calculated to gull the un-
wary farmer; and you will observe the “ Favourite’’ is
not spelled in the American way. . =

Throughout this large advertisement there is nothing
to indicate where the mowers are made, and with

THE RETURN OF DOUGLAS HYDE.

R. DOUGLAS HYDE got a right royal welcome-
back to Ireland, and after his seven months’
hard labour in America in the interests of the Irish-
Ireland idea, he got no more than he well deserved.
Ilis American tour appears to have been an unqualified
success, and on his return he appropriately was accorded

a triumphal entry into the metropolis. s
The editorial columns of the /rish Z'umes kept saying
nothing. Doubtless, also, the ex-people’s William reads
with a sort of longing of the reception accorded to the
_President of the Gaelic League, for William is very
fond of cheers. But the old order is giving way to the
mnew. X
As President of the Gaelic League, Douglas Hyde has
had a difficult part to play, and the onérous.position

develops. One thing to be thankful for is that in all
Ireland he has no competitor for the post. It is an
old standing view of ours that it is the particular
balance and arrangement of qualities rather than the
possession of a number of remarkable qualities that go
to maks the effective man for any given work.. Dr.
Hyde has happily many brilliant qualities, -but it
is his unique suitability for the unique position he holds
that constitutes his great strength, and that makes for
the stability of the organisatien over which he presides.
‘“An Cpaobin, fresh from his American tour, is im- °
pressed with the view that Ireland’s battle must be
fought out in Ireland.  This certainly coincides with
our own view, though we have not been to America.
Wa have done sometliing by way of turning ridicule on
the raimels about- twenty millions of our race abroad,’’
and that sort of thing. We have no faith in a “ greater
Ireland beyond the . The Irish in England, in
America, and in Aust except those of them who may
return to Ireland, we luok upon as lost practically to e
country for ever, though for the time being their poli-
tical, moral and financial aid for some specific purposes
are of value, and can be utilised to advantage. - But
at the present stage of our.history Ireland is the only
site in the world upon which an Irish nation can be
erected. Sixty years ago perhaps the formation of an
Trish-speaking Republic, similar to the late soer Re-
publics-in South Africa, might have been possible—but.
all that now is a might-have-been. Douglas Hyde sees
s that there 1s no hope for Irish in the United
In the course of his reply to the address pre-
F d to him in Queenstown, he said:—“ Of cowrse, in
the States there was no use for the Irish language, nor,
indecd, for any obher language except English.  Even
the Germans were finding it hard to keop their language
alive in the States as a spoken tongue. Even in Cin-
cinabbi, the most German city in America, with a German
populdtion of 400,000, where German was taught in
every school in-the city, they could not keep it alive.
So it would be impossible to keep Irish alive there.”
Hoilaid great stress on the important fact that he
was alilo to saj in America that the Language Movement -
was cdnfined o no class, or creed, or political belief.
“Tf I had not been able to tell them that,” said he, “I
might a well have stopped at home.” . He said very
noatly that the Gaelic Leaguo stands for all Irishmen
except :\l)xbi‘-Irishmeu ;5 and in his speech in Dublin
, .

@

e

- again returned to the point—*But I would not have

been able to make any impression upon America if I
had not been able to assure them that tho Gaclic League
stood apart and far above all petty politics.” It is cvi-
dent that the President is keenly alive to the danger of
narrowing the League in any way, and it is in the
manner in which, so far, he has kept out of all con-
troversies involying class, creed and party Irish politics
that he has displayed his greatest tact.  Though it
would be death to the Gaelic League if it were captured
by, and made a mere plank of, the United Irish
League, yot, on the other hand, the members of the
United Irish League and the members of the
Il'lsh_ Parliamentary Party should not be given any
genuine reason to feel that the Gaolic Leaguo was, as
su_ch, hostile to them. The work of the language is to
Irishise all in the country, whatever their erced, class, or
politics, and though there arc a large number, particu-
laa_‘ly in the North, who we do not believe will bo
Inishised in this or the next generation, or the one after
that, yet no one would welcome them into the Irish
ranks more cordially than we if they choose to come.
. But within the great boundaries of Irish Ireland there
is room, not only for individuality, but for any num-
ber of various parties and organisations, and it would
be as great an evil to attempt to suppress individuality
or distinct parties as it would be to attempt to identify
the movement with any one party. The members of
the Irish Parliamentary Party, of the United Irish
League, of Cuma n na naeveal, of the Protestant
Thousand, of the Catholic Defence Society, of the Coun-
cil over which Mr. Edward Martyn presides, and the
members of any amount of other bodies have a right to
be tolerated within the Gaelic League so long as indivi-
dually they are Gaelic Leaguers. A Protestant does not
give up any of his particular religious views when he joins
the Gaelic League, and no one has a right to object to
a Protestant Gaelic Leaguer as such should he choose, in
his individual capacity, to attempt to prove that the
Pope has cloven hoofs; and neither does a Catholic cease
to look at things from a Catholic point of view when he
joins the Gaelic League. TFor our own part, so long as
we think it is called for, we will fight vigorously for the
dzfence of the secular rights of Catholics in this country,
no matter how many “ non-sectarian ’’ bodies we may
belong to. Any kind of over-emphasised “all creeds,
all classes”” arrangement that would tend to produce a
merely nondescript class containing two parties, the
members of each of which were afraid to say in their
individual capacities what they honestly thought—only
one party being much more timid than the other—is
not a desirable arrangement. Let us all blaze away out-
¢ide to our heart’s content, but keep the compact honestly
whilst on the platform of the Gaelic League. Thero is
room in the League for all classes and conditions of
Irishmen—anti-Irishmen of course are outsiders—and
the chief danger is not in diversity, but rather in any
attempts at setting up political, social, or other ex-
traneous tests.

The Gaelic League can never be expected to glide
along as easily as a motor car. There will be friction
here and there now and again. Even the most obscure
branch of the League occupies in many respects such a
delicate position that its guidance often calls for a dis-
play of great tact; and deposits of tact are not univer-
sal. Questions of the individual right to play this gamo
or that, local controversies with clergymen are fre-
quently matters charged with inflammable stuff, and if
the local tact is not equal to the issue, at least if thero
are unpleasantnesses let them he localised as much as
possible.

Dr. Hyde’s great success as Irish-Ireland Ambassador
to thz United States will tend perhaps to add to his heavy
personal burdens as President of the CGlaelic League,
for the country will be expecting even more from him
than it did previous to his visit. ~The movement in
which his is the most outstanding personality is only
in its infancy yet, and there are big struggles hefore it
with the Briton in and out of Ireland. The greater tho

success the keemer will' be the opposition. We were glad
to note a fighting ring in all the speeches of An Cpaobin

B

‘and his sojourn amongst the breezy Americans evideatly
“hag done his heart good.

THOUGHTS ON SOME PRESENT DISCONTENTS.

OME months ago in the articles T contributed to
tho Leaper on “ Bigotry in Belfast,”” T traced
roughl‘y tho dovelopment of the present pqlibic:»l situa-
gion in Ulster known as the North-cast difficulty. In
doing so, I took occasion to say what I thought of the
Ulstor Prosbyterians as Irishmen. I gave them every
credit for what they did, and for what they were will-
ing should be done, for _Ix'cland as a whole during the
period of 1778-97. Having done that, I was ‘J(Jl.lfl'()nf,ed
with the question which puzzled Gladstone himself—
why it is that the descendants of those Presbyterian
patriots of a hundred years ago.lmvc, ever since the
Union, been for England and against Ireland? I gave
it as my opinion that the reason was that these patriots
nover were Irish, and never want to be.‘ That their
cbullition during the Volunteer era was sxmply a pout
caused by England’s ill-treatment of them. That even
at that time they would not have been willing to eman-
cipate the mere Irish but for the fact that the mere
Trish were, and had been for close on a century, so
utterly out of the question as a pphtlcal force that
nobody could think of them as nursing any longer any
ambition to be ever again the Irish nation. In a word,
I argued that the Presbyterian Planter, though at times
capable of more generous sentiments than the Episco-
palian, was still a Planter, and, as soon as he saw t}lc
ancient race showing signs of life once more, and a dis-
position to look after its inheritance, fell back again
for safety into the arms of England, and has never
sinco been prevailed upon to trust himself out of them.

These arguments of mine, and the whole North-east
qusstion, have been brought back to my mind by cer-
tain ovents that have since taken place in Belfast.
The General Election was one of these events.  The
result of that election in the West and South divisions
has been hailed by the Nationalists and proclaimed by
them to the world as proof that the “ Hands across the
Boyne’’ ideal is now almost an accomplished fact.
The Unionist Press of Belfast, on the other hand, is at
present if anything more unanimously black and bitter
against Ireland than ever it was, and yet its circulation
doesn’t seem to be going down.  Speaking as one who
would like to see the hands linked across the Boyne, 1
must say that I don't myself notice any great desire
on the part of the Planters about me to do their part
of the linking: Nor am I surprised. ~ Why should
they? Guiding myself by the principles I have laid
down as having ruled their development as a body, 1
can easily put myself in their place, and, in their place,
I don’t think I would be in any hurry to vote for Home
Rule. The Orangeman is well enough off as he is, and,
his history having been what it is, he fears naturally
cnough, even if he had no News Letter nor 12th July
oratory to make him fear, that he might not be so well
off under Home Rule. Nor is there any use in arguing
with him. All that we can do is to strengthen our own
side, and get Homa Rule in spite of him, giving him,
howover, any conditions he likes short of separation
from us. I say short of separation for two reasons.
One reason is that it would be a tremendously compli-
cated business, even in “ Chanel’s © hands, to arrangs
the separation deed, and, after all, it would be labour
lost, because as soon as Home Rule would have proved
a success—which, of course, it would—your Orangeman
would not get a night’s rest until he would be back again
to us, and would have taken out his naturalisation papers
as an Irishman. My other reason is perhaps a merely
personal one. Not to speak of the historic associations
and the lovely scenery of much of the North-cast corner,
under a separation arrangement I would myself have
to give up my present business, or else remain under
English rule.

By the way, though I can’t say that I feel uncom-
fot:hqhlo ab finding myself in conflict in a matter “of
opinion with our Nationalist politicians, it occurs to
mo to noto as strange the fact that they persist in think-
ing that the Planter can be made an Irishman before
we geti Flome Rule, which is entirely a matter for him—
while they argue with evident conviction that we can't
save the Inish language or industries without Home

The Castleknock Chroniele still holds the fort against

San Francisco, June 11th, 1906

Irish-Americans all over the Continent as well as out
lore agree that the only independent newspaper pub-
lished in Ireland is the LEaDER. Its support of Irish-
made goods, its advocacy of Ireland for the Irish, and
its fearless denunciation of bigotry and hypocrisy is re-
freshing after the long years of undisputed sway which
tho old mosshack organs enjoyed, whose policy was to
agreo with everything right or wrong when it would
cost them a few subscribers or advertisers.

My present reason for writing is on account of the
stand your able journal has taken on temperance, and
what great work has been done through temperance
during our great calamity out here is a lesson that will
for ever stand greater than human eloquence could pic-
ture for its advocacy.

The earthquake and fire was the greatest in the
world’s history. I will not attempt to give you a de-
tailed description of the horrors or incidents, nor to my
mind can any one writer. Lytton’s description of the
“Last Days of Pompeii,”” and all other sensational
writers of fiction could not over-estimate the many
thrilling things that occurred for the three days suc-
ceeding the 18th of April. I will give your readers a
slight idea of the area of fire by imagining that all
Dublin was burned with the exception of the upper part
of Rathmines and Rathgar, and little strips were saved
at Inchicore and some of the residence places at Sandy-
mount and Merrion. And now to give a little idea of
the loss. The insurance companies are liable for
300,000,000 dollars (60 million pounds), which is only
supposed (o represent less than one-third actual damage,
as many were not insured at all, a great many for less
than half, and none for the whole value of property, so
it is safe to say that a billion dollars, or £200,000,000 -
would be near the total loss. This was caused by the
carth shake at 5.15 in the morning, which tumbled
down many fires and lamps which were lighted and
causing about twenby-two fires in different parts at the
same time, leaving one of the most efficient fire depart-
ments in the world almost useless on account of the
bursting of watering mains and the killing of their gal-
lant chief early in the morning.

The sight, then, of fire in all directions was like as if
hell was let loose. Buildings, both small and great, fell
before the fury of the flames like houses of cards, and
tho horror of terror-stricken inhabitants, rich and poor,
old, young and infirm, leaving their homes, going from
one place to another, and again being pursued by the
dovastating fires, was fearful, and can only be appre-,
clated by an actual looker-on.

And with all the excitement, troubles, and priva-
tions such as the world never before witnessed, good
order provailed. And what might you ask was the
reason? The answer is ¢ Temperance’’ (in big letters).
When the fire commenced the civil and military autho-
rities gave orders to have every saloon or publichouse
closed, and the military went round the same time and
broke overy bottle and spilled every drop of liquor in
barrels in the streets, and it was almost death penalty

What is the lesson which I said we can in Dublin

rary buildings and the great energy of the 60,000 arti-

You might ask what is my idea in writing this. T

visiting the land of my birth (Ireland, which I love so
much) last year, and was often ashamed of foreigners
seeing the sights of dirt and drunkenness in the public
streets of large towns, and especially the beautiful city
of Dublin, and the matter of fact way the respectable
-element regarded the every-day occurrence. I would
be the last in the world to say unkind words of my
countrymen and women, but you cannot see such spec-
tacles in any part of the world as you can see in the

What a blessing if Father Mathew again arose, or
that the Church took up the cause of temperance and
that the thirteen or fourteen million pounds spent on
drink, or at least say ten millions, were turned to food

and are better paid, and can with all that do without
stimulants and do their work much better. The people
of San Francisco are cosmopolitan, though a great many
are Irish, and the majority of the population are sup-
posed to be Catholic. I listened some years ago to a
Jecture from our eloquent Archbishop Riordan. It was
on temperance, and the most prpmiuenb professors,

in a fearless way he wound up in the end by 'asl(mg
Catholic saloon keepers to quit the business, and if even
they had to work in sewers for a living to do it. I omly

Assuming that Father O’Connor is more serious than

I hope it will have been guessed that when I speak

times taught a class to-night, and a Waterford man the

actors were never so serious. No Irish was learned
except by those who learned it at home from the books,
practising the speaking of it as opportunity offered, and

most was the Irish-speaking district during each annual
holiday, and—which is the point I wish to bring out—
the same district for the same learner every holiday. 1
don’t think I know anybody who has learned Irish
even fairly well who has not done so by sticking from
the start to one particular style of Irish and to one
particular district in which to spend his holidays.

It will be said that this is the dialect question brought
up again. Well, call it what you like, it is a live ques-
tion. If your teacher is a Munster man you must learn
to speak Munster Irish or none. If he is an Ulster man

former case, if you go to Arran for your holidays, it

your spoken Irish. In the other case, you might as well
go to Island Magee for your holidays as to Ring, in the
County Waterford. And so on. Not that good speakers
from the various districts would have any trouble in
understanding each other, for they would not. Indeed,
they would learn from each other as fast just as they
could speak, and so would improve each other’s Irish.
